MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the key prior systems used on these datasets (Mishra et al. variants for sentiment and sarcasm and the Joshi dataset), and includes sensible in-paper baselines (CNN text-only) evaluated on the same data with the same metric (accuracy). The authors explicitly acknowledge when their gaze-augmented model yields only a small, statistically insignificant gain, and they report hyperparameters and dataset details, suggesting fair conditions. However, the evaluation appears to omit broader or more recent neural baselines and deeper ablation analyses that would strengthen claims about the contribution of learned gaze features, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper evaluates against appropriate prior systems (Mishra et al. series for sentiment and sarcasm; Joshi et al. dataset) and also includes strong internal baselines (CNN text-only variants) using the same datasets and accuracy metric, so comparisons are largely fair. The authors candidly acknowledge that adding learned gaze features yields only a small, statistically insignificant boost for sarcasm, and they report hyperparameter and experimental details. From my cognitive-NLP perspective, the work could be stronger by comparing to more recent neural sarcasm/sentiment models and releasing code for reproducibility, but overall the comparisons are good with only minor omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against the key prior systems used on these datasets (Mishra et al. variants for sentiment and sarcasm and the Joshi dataset), and includes sensible in-paper baselines (CNN text-only) evaluated on the same data with the same metric (accuracy). The authors explicitly acknowledge when their gaze-augmented model yields only a small, statistically insignificant gain, and they report hyperparameters and dataset details, suggesting fair conditions. However, the evaluation appears to omit broader or more recent neural baselines and deeper ablation analyses that would strengthen claims about the contribution of learned gaze features, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against appropriate prior systems (Mishra et al. series for sentiment and sarcasm; Joshi et al. dataset) and also includes strong internal baselines (CNN text-only variants) using the same datasets and accuracy metric, so comparisons are largely fair. The authors candidly acknowledge that adding learned gaze features yields only a small, statistically insignificant boost for sarcasm, and they report hyperparameter and experimental details. From my cognitive-NLP perspective, the work could be stronger by comparing to more recent neural sarcasm/sentiment models and releasing code for reproducibility, but overall the comparisons are good with only minor omissions.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles a meaningful problem—integrating cognitive gaze signals into NLP classifiers for sentiment and sarcasm—and demonstrates that a CNN can learn useful representations from combined text+gaze inputs, with up to 11.27% accuracy gain over traditional handcrafted systems on sarcasm. However, the added benefit of the gaze component over a text-only CNN is small (1.36%, statistically insignificant) and the evaluation relies on two small, reader-limited datasets (994 and 843 snippets), which constrains generalizability. As a reproducible-ML engineer, I view this as a useful, incremental contribution that validates a promising multimodal direction but is unlikely to substantially change practice without larger-scale, more conclusive evidence and released code. | B(3): This paper tackles an important intersection—automatically learning cognitive (gaze) features and integrating them with CNNs for sentiment and sarcasm detection—which is relevant to both NLP and psycholinguistics. However, the practical impact is moderate: while the method outperforms previous handcrafted-feature systems (notably up to 11.27% on sarcasm), the explicit gaze component yields only a small, statistically insignificant 1.36% gain over text-only CNNs, and the experiments use small, reader-limited datasets (7 and 5 readers). Thus the work is useful and likely to stimulate follow-up work on learned cognitive features and better data collection, but it is unlikely to be transformative without stronger evidence of substantive gains or broader, more representative gaze corpora.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful problem—integrating cognitive gaze signals into NLP classifiers for sentiment and sarcasm—and demonstrates that a CNN can learn useful representations from combined text+gaze inputs, with up to 11.27% accuracy gain over traditional handcrafted systems on sarcasm. However, the added benefit of the gaze component over a text-only CNN is small (1.36%, statistically insignificant) and the evaluation relies on two small, reader-limited datasets (994 and 843 snippets), which constrains generalizability. As a reproducible-ML engineer, I view this as a useful, incremental contribution that validates a promising multimodal direction but is unlikely to substantially change practice without larger-scale, more conclusive evidence and released code.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important intersection—automatically learning cognitive (gaze) features and integrating them with CNNs for sentiment and sarcasm detection—which is relevant to both NLP and psycholinguistics. However, the practical impact is moderate: while the method outperforms previous handcrafted-feature systems (notably up to 11.27% on sarcasm), the explicit gaze component yields only a small, statistically insignificant 1.36% gain over text-only CNNs, and the experiments use small, reader-limited datasets (7 and 5 readers). Thus the work is useful and likely to stimulate follow-up work on learned cognitive features and better data collection, but it is unlikely to be transformative without stronger evidence of substantive gains or broader, more representative gaze corpora.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a topical NLP/ML problem—combining cognitive (gaze) signals with neural text models for sentiment and sarcasm detection—which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The methodology (CNNs over text and 2D gaze inputs) and comparisons to prior handcrafted-feature baselines are appropriate, but concerns remain about small datasets (994 and 843 snippets), reliance on accuracy-only reporting, a statistically insignificant gaze benefit for sarcasm, and limited reproducibility (no code mentioned and hyper-parameters set by trial-and-error). Given those reproducibility and evaluation shortcomings, the work is a good fit but would benefit from stronger experimental rigor to make it a perfect match. | B(4): This paper sits squarely in the intersection of NLP and cognitive signal integration and thus fits well within top NLP/ML venues: it proposes a neural architecture to learn gaze-derived features and evaluates on sentiment and sarcasm benchmarks with appropriate baselines. The methodology and evaluation (CNN variants, comparisons to prior handcrafted gaze/text systems) meet community standards, but the datasets are small and the reported gains from adding gaze (notably only a 1.36% statistically insignificant boost for sarcasm) suggest a more incremental contribution. Overall it is relevant to the venue audience, though the limited empirical impact and dataset scale are minor concerns about broader significance.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a topical NLP/ML problem—combining cognitive (gaze) signals with neural text models for sentiment and sarcasm detection—which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The methodology (CNNs over text and 2D gaze inputs) and comparisons to prior handcrafted-feature baselines are appropriate, but concerns remain about small datasets (994 and 843 snippets), reliance on accuracy-only reporting, a statistically insignificant gaze benefit for sarcasm, and limited reproducibility (no code mentioned and hyper-parameters set by trial-and-error). Given those reproducibility and evaluation shortcomings, the work is a good fit but would benefit from stronger experimental rigor to make it a perfect match.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely in the intersection of NLP and cognitive signal integration and thus fits well within top NLP/ML venues: it proposes a neural architecture to learn gaze-derived features and evaluates on sentiment and sarcasm benchmarks with appropriate baselines. The methodology and evaluation (CNN variants, comparisons to prior handcrafted gaze/text systems) meet community standards, but the datasets are small and the reported gains from adding gaze (notably only a 1.36% statistically insignificant boost for sarcasm) suggest a more incremental contribution. Overall it is relevant to the venue audience, though the limited empirical impact and dataset scale are minor concerns about broader significance.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear experimental claims and compares against appropriate baselines (handcrafted Mishra systems and text-only CNNs) and reports gains (up to 11.27% on sarcasm), but the key improvement from adding gaze to the CNN is small (1.36%) and reported as statistically insignificant, suggesting limited empirical support for the central claim. Mathematical content is minimal and appears consistent, but experimental methodology has gaps: small datasets with few readers, no mention of code release, and unclear whether proper statistical testing/confidence intervals were applied beyond the stated insignificance. Hyperparameter selection by trial-and-error and lack of ablation detail on the contribution of each modality leave reproducibility and attribution of gains uncertain. Overall, the work is promising but has methodological weaknesses that prevent full technical validation. | B(4): The paper's principal experimental claims are largely supported: CNN text+gaze models outperform text-only CNNs and prior handcrafted-feature systems on the provided sentiment and sarcasm datasets, and the authors honestly report the gaze component yields only a small, statistically insignificant gain on sarcasm. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental setup compares against appropriate baselines; however, details about statistical testing are sparse (significance is only reported for the small sarcasm gain) and hyperparameter selection via trial-and-error and small dataset sizes weaken confidence in generality. Overall the methodology is sound with minor concerns about robustness and clarity of statistical analysis, so the technical assessment is positive but not flawless.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear experimental claims and compares against appropriate baselines (handcrafted Mishra systems and text-only CNNs) and reports gains (up to 11.27% on sarcasm), but the key improvement from adding gaze to the CNN is small (1.36%) and reported as statistically insignificant, suggesting limited empirical support for the central claim. Mathematical content is minimal and appears consistent, but experimental methodology has gaps: small datasets with few readers, no mention of code release, and unclear whether proper statistical testing/confidence intervals were applied beyond the stated insignificance. Hyperparameter selection by trial-and-error and lack of ablation detail on the contribution of each modality leave reproducibility and attribution of gains uncertain. Overall, the work is promising but has methodological weaknesses that prevent full technical validation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's principal experimental claims are largely supported: CNN text+gaze models outperform text-only CNNs and prior handcrafted-feature systems on the provided sentiment and sarcasm datasets, and the authors honestly report the gaze component yields only a small, statistically insignificant gain on sarcasm. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental setup compares against appropriate baselines; however, details about statistical testing are sparse (significance is only reported for the small sarcasm gain) and hyperparameter selection via trial-and-error and small dataset sizes weaken confidence in generality. Overall the methodology is sound with minor concerns about robustness and clarity of statistical analysis, so the technical assessment is positive but not flawless.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies a CNN-based joint text+gaze representation to sentiment and sarcasm classification, which clearly differentiates itself from prior work that relied on handcrafted gaze features (Mishra et al.). However, the core modeling approach (CNNs over embeddings and 2D gaze signals) is an incremental application of standard deep architectures rather than a fundamentally new method, and the reported gains from adding gaze are small or statistically insignificant for sarcasm. From a reproducibility and novelty standpoint, this reads as a moderate, empirically-driven extension rather than a highly original conceptual advance. | B(3): The paper applies a standard CNN architecture to learn representations from 2D gaze data and fuse them with text for sentiment and sarcasm classification, which is a reasonable but technically incremental shift from prior work that used handcrafted gaze features (Mishra et al.). The problem itself (sentiment/sarcasm with gaze signals) is established in the literature and the authors largely adapt existing neural techniques rather than proposing a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. The manuscript does clearly differentiate its contribution (automatic learned gaze features vs. handcrafted features) and reports modest empirical gains, but the methodological novelty beyond applying convolutional feature learning to gaze inputs is limited, so the contribution is moderate rather than highly original.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies a CNN-based joint text+gaze representation to sentiment and sarcasm classification, which clearly differentiates itself from prior work that relied on handcrafted gaze features (Mishra et al.). However, the core modeling approach (CNNs over embeddings and 2D gaze signals) is an incremental application of standard deep architectures rather than a fundamentally new method, and the reported gains from adding gaze are small or statistically insignificant for sarcasm. From a reproducibility and novelty standpoint, this reads as a moderate, empirically-driven extension rather than a highly original conceptual advance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies a standard CNN architecture to learn representations from 2D gaze data and fuse them with text for sentiment and sarcasm classification, which is a reasonable but technically incremental shift from prior work that used handcrafted gaze features (Mishra et al.). The problem itself (sentiment/sarcasm with gaze signals) is established in the literature and the authors largely adapt existing neural techniques rather than proposing a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. The manuscript does clearly differentiate its contribution (automatic learned gaze features vs. handcrafted features) and reports modest empirical gains, but the methodological novelty beyond applying convolutional feature learning to gaze inputs is limited, so the contribution is moderate rather than highly original.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates its CNN text+gaze model on two existing datasets and compares against prior handcrafted-feature systems and text-only CNNs, which supports the central claim that learned cognitive features can help; however the reported gains from adding gaze for sarcasm are small and statistically insignificant, and no thorough sensitivity analyses or systematic ablations (e.g., contribution of each gaze component, reader-level variability, or hyperparameter searches) are presented. Related work appears adequately covered and some limitations (small gain, ad-hoc hyperparameter tuning) are acknowledged, but key reproducibility elements are missing or weakly reported (no code mentioned, limited discussion of random seeds/hardware, and datasets are small). Overall the experiments are adequate to show potential but lack the depth and rigorous ablations/statistical analysis needed for strong, reproducible claims. | B(3): The experiments are adequate to show that learned text+gaze features can outperform prior handcrafted systems and text-only CNNs on the provided datasets, and they report datasets, baselines, and hyperparameters. However, the analysis lacks deeper ablations/sensitivity studies (e.g., how much each gaze component contributes across datasets) and tuning was left informal, which weakens claims about the gaze module’s utility—particularly since the sarcasm gain was small and not significant. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and limitations (small gaze benefit, hyperparameter issues) are acknowledged, but failure cases and reproducibility (no code mentioned despite only hyperparameters reported) are not fully addressed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates its CNN text+gaze model on two existing datasets and compares against prior handcrafted-feature systems and text-only CNNs, which supports the central claim that learned cognitive features can help; however the reported gains from adding gaze for sarcasm are small and statistically insignificant, and no thorough sensitivity analyses or systematic ablations (e.g., contribution of each gaze component, reader-level variability, or hyperparameter searches) are presented. Related work appears adequately covered and some limitations (small gain, ad-hoc hyperparameter tuning) are acknowledged, but key reproducibility elements are missing or weakly reported (no code mentioned, limited discussion of random seeds/hardware, and datasets are small). Overall the experiments are adequate to show potential but lack the depth and rigorous ablations/statistical analysis needed for strong, reproducible claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are adequate to show that learned text+gaze features can outperform prior handcrafted systems and text-only CNNs on the provided datasets, and they report datasets, baselines, and hyperparameters. However, the analysis lacks deeper ablations/sensitivity studies (e.g., how much each gaze component contributes across datasets) and tuning was left informal, which weakens claims about the gaze module’s utility—particularly since the sarcasm gain was small and not significant. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and limitations (small gaze benefit, hyperparameter issues) are acknowledged, but failure cases and reproducibility (no code mentioned despite only hyperparameters reported) are not fully addressed.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with the standard flow (introduction, method, experiments, conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (learning cognitive features from gaze and combining them with text for sentiment/sarcasm). Notation and hyperparameter reporting are adequate for reproducibility claims in the summary, and related work coverage is noted as adequate. Although writing quality is described as good, the modest and statistically insignificant improvement for gaze in sarcasm suggests some experimental clarity issues; figures/tables and examples are not explicitly described in the summary, so I rate presentation as strong overall but with minor gaps that could be clarified (e.g., explicit figure/table effectiveness and any remaining ambiguities). | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early, presenting methodology, datasets, baselines, and reported limitations (e.g., small/insignificant gaze benefit for sarcasm, fixed hyper‑parameters). Notation and hyper‑parameter reporting are described, and related work coverage is adequate, contributing to overall readability. Minor presentation issues remain — the practical interpretation of the small gaze benefit and choices around hyper‑parameter tuning could be more explicitly discussed — but overall the writing quality is good and the paper is easy to follow.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with the standard flow (introduction, method, experiments, conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (learning cognitive features from gaze and combining them with text for sentiment/sarcasm). Notation and hyperparameter reporting are adequate for reproducibility claims in the summary, and related work coverage is noted as adequate. Although writing quality is described as good, the modest and statistically insignificant improvement for gaze in sarcasm suggests some experimental clarity issues; figures/tables and examples are not explicitly described in the summary, so I rate presentation as strong overall but with minor gaps that could be clarified (e.g., explicit figure/table effectiveness and any remaining ambiguities).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early, presenting methodology, datasets, baselines, and reported limitations (e.g., small/insignificant gaze benefit for sarcasm, fixed hyper‑parameters). Notation and hyper‑parameter reporting are described, and related work coverage is adequate, contributing to overall readability. Minor presentation issues remain — the practical interpretation of the small gaze benefit and choices around hyper‑parameter tuning could be more explicitly discussed — but overall the writing quality is good and the paper is easy to follow.

main_claims: Introduces a CNN-based framework to automatically learn cognitive features from eye-movement (gaze) data and combine them with textual features for sentiment polarity and sarcasm detection.
Using learned text+gaze features yields better classification performance than (i) CNN systems using text alone and (ii) existing systems that rely on handcrafted gaze and textual features.

On the sarcasm detection task, their CNN variants outperform traditional systems by up to 11.27% accuracy; however, adding the gaze component to the CNN yields only a 1.36% (statistically insignificant) improvement over text-only CNN.
_summary.methodology: A convolutional neural network with separate components for processing text and gaze inputs is trained to learn feature representations from pre-trained word embeddings and 2D gaze data (using convolutional filters, pooling, dropout and ADADELTA optimizer) and used to classify sentiment polarity and sarcasm.
datasets: Dataset 1 (released by Mishra et al., 2016a) — 994 snippets (383 positive, 611 negative), 350 sarcastic; eye-movement data from 7 readers per snippet.
Dataset 2 (used by Joshi et al., 2014) — 843 snippets (443 positive, 400 negative), eye-movement data from 5 readers per snippet.
baselines: Mishra et al. (2016c) systems (handcrafted text and gaze features) for sentiment analysis
Mishra et al. (2016b) sarcasm classifier (for sarcasm detection)
CNN variants using text component alone
limitations: Improvement from adding the gaze component to the CNN for sarcasm detection is small (1.36%) and statistically insignificant.
Hyper-parameters were fixed by trial and error and may not be optimal; tuning is left to future work.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - sentiment and sarcasm classification using cognitive (gaze) data

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible ML Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior machine learning engineer with a PhD in NLP focusing on model evaluation and reliable experimental methodology; has led reproducibility audits and implemented productionized neural classifiers for academic and industry datasets.
traits: insists on clear baseline definitions, hyperparameter details, and training/validation splits to enable replication
looks for ablation studies that justify architectural choices and measure the contribution of each input modality
evaluates statistical validity of reported improvements and requests appropriate significance testing or confidence intervals
values transparent reporting of preprocessing, data selection, and implementation details (random seeds, hardware, runtime)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I prioritize rigorous experimental design and reproducibility: I verify that claims are supported by proper baselines, ablations, and statistically sound evaluation so that results can be trusted and reproduced.

reviewer_b.name: Computational Psycholinguist
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor working at the intersection of NLP and cognitive science, with a track record of papers using behavioral signals (eye-tracking, EEG) to improve language understanding and pragmatic inference models.
traits: examines how cognitive signals are integrated with linguistic models and whether the integration is theoretically and practically meaningful
assesses novelty relative to prior cognitive-NLP work and whether the paper moves the field forward in using behavioral data
values clarity about real-world applicability, dataset representativeness, and the interpretability of learned cognitive features
notices whether findings advance understanding of when and why cognitive signals help (e.g., specific phenomena like sarcasm) rather than only reporting aggregate gains
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I judge whether the work provides a substantive, novel contribution to combining cognitive signals with NLP models and whether the experiments and analysis illuminate the mechanisms and impact of using gaze data.
